narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 95.8% chance that you would not pass. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which stood out as the top factor pushing toward this failure prediction—it's well below the typical 6th decile among students who passed, signaling weaker performance relative to peers at that stage. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 2nd, another early low ranking compared to the average 6th decile for bar passers, which further reinforced the model's concern about your academic trajectory. Your LSAT score of 28 also contributed notably toward the failure prediction, falling short of the 37 average seen in successful students and highlighting a gap in standardized readiness. While your middle family income aligns with about a third of bar passers and your choice to study full-time matches the vast majority of them, these didn't outweigh the dominant academic signals. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, likewise below the 3.24 average for passers, played a supporting role but wasn't as pivotal as those top three factors. In essence, the model saw your consistently lower decile rankings and LSAT as red flags when stacked against profiles of admitted students who typically show stronger academic deciles and test scores from the start. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven forecast aimed at bar success rates. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades if possible through additional coursework, or exploring programs with bridge support for students building their academic profiles. You might also seek feedback from admissions counselors on targeted improvements or apply to schools with holistic reviews that weigh your full-time commitment and personal drive more heavily. With focused steps, you can reshape these metrics and strengthen your next application.
